facts.text: Alfred Hitchcock's Psycho (1960) was the first American film to show a toilet flushing on screen, which was considered shocking at the time.
The Blair Witch Project (1999) was made for roughly $60,000 and grossed nearly $250 million worldwide, making it one of the most profitable films ever.
George A. Romero's Night of the Living Dead was released on October 1, 1968, and essentially invented the modern zombie genre.
The chainsaw used in The Texas Chain Saw Massacre (1974) was a real, functioning chainsaw with the chain removed for safety during close-up shots.
In the Japanese horror film Ringu (1998), the actress who played Sadako walked backward and the footage was reversed to create her unnatural movement.
Jordan Peele's Get Out (2017) was made for just $4.5 million and earned over $255 million worldwide.
The Walking Dead premiered on AMC on October 31, 2010 — Halloween night — and became the most-watched drama series in basic cable history.
Tom Savini, legendary makeup effects artist, served as a combat photographer in Vietnam, and his wartime experiences influenced his realistic gore effects.
The mask worn by Michael Myers in Halloween (1978) was actually a Captain Kirk mask from Star Trek, painted white with altered eye holes.
Hereditary (2018) director Ari Aster built a detailed miniature of the house used in the film, and actress Toni Collette's character is a miniature artist.
The Exorcist was released on December 26, 1973, and audiences reportedly fainted, vomited, and fled theaters during screenings.
Wes Craven named Freddy Krueger after a kid who bullied him in school, combining the name with that of a real-life child killer he read about.
The found footage subgenre traces its roots to Cannibal Holocaust (1980), whose director was arrested because authorities believed the deaths were real.
Paranormal Activity (2007) was made for approximately $15,000 and earned over $193 million at the global box office.
John Carpenter composed the iconic Halloween theme himself in just three days because he couldn't afford to hire a composer.
The Conjuring (2013) is based on the real-life case files of paranormal investigators Ed and Lorraine Warren.
American Horror Story debuted on FX on October 5, 2011, and each season tells a self-contained story with a recurring ensemble cast.
The term 'final girl' was coined by film professor Carol Clover in her 1992 book Men, Women, and Chain Saws to describe the last surviving female in slasher films.
Italian giallo films of the 1960s and 70s, known for stylish violence and mystery plots, heavily influenced the American slasher genre.
Rick Baker won the first-ever Academy Award for Best Makeup for An American Werewolf in London (1981).
Stranger Things premiered on Netflix on July 15, 2016, and its blend of 1980s nostalgia and horror became a global phenomenon.
The Babadook (2014) became an unlikely LGBTQ+ icon after a Netflix categorization glitch placed it under LGBT movies, and fans embraced the meme.
In A Quiet Place (2018), there are only about 90 lines of dialogue in the entire film, with most communication done through American Sign Language.
The original Friday the 13th (1980) does not feature Jason as the killer — his mother, Pamela Voorhees, is the antagonist.
Tobe Hooper directed The Texas Chain Saw Massacre in extreme Texas heat, and the cast and crew suffered from actual heat exhaustion during filming.
The Saw franchise has grossed over $1 billion worldwide across its installments, making it one of the most successful horror franchises of all time.
Mike Flanagan's The Haunting of Hill House (2018) features a hidden ghost in nearly every frame of certain episodes that most viewers don't notice.
The Witch (2015), also styled as The VVitch, used only natural lighting and candles to achieve its period-accurate 1630s New England look.
In Scream (1996), Drew Barrymore was originally cast as the lead Sidney Prescott but asked to play Casey Becker instead so her early death would shock audiences.
Nosferatu (1922) was an unauthorized adaptation of Bram Stoker's Dracula, and a court ordered all copies destroyed, though some survived.
Korean horror film Train to Busan (2016) was praised for reinventing the zombie genre with its intense action and emotional depth.
The Conjuring universe has grossed over $2 billion worldwide, making it the highest-grossing horror franchise in history.
James Wan directed both The Conjuring and Insidious, establishing himself as one of the most commercially successful horror directors of the 21st century.
Night of the Living Dead (1968) was one of the first American films to feature a Black actor, Duane Jones, in the lead role, which was revolutionary for its time.
The French extremity movement, including films like Martyrs (2008) and Inside (2007), pushed horror into graphic, confrontational territory.
Halloween (1978) was released on October 25, 1978, and was made for just $325,000 but earned over $70 million worldwide.
Child's Play (1988) was inspired by the real legend of Robert the Doll, a supposedly haunted doll kept in a museum in Key West, Florida.
Jordan Peele became the first Black writer-director to earn over $100 million at the domestic box office with Get Out.
The practical creature effects in John Carpenter's The Thing (1982) were created by Rob Bottin, who was only 22 years old and hospitalized from exhaustion after filming.
Midsommar (2019) was filmed almost entirely in broad daylight, subverting the horror convention that scary things happen in the dark.
The iconic shower scene in Psycho took seven days to shoot, used 70 camera angles, and featured chocolate syrup as blood.
Yellowjackets premiered on Showtime on November 14, 2021, blending survival drama with psychological horror across two timelines.
Final Destination (2000) was originally written as a spec script for The X-Files before being developed into a standalone film.
The Evil Dead (1981) was filmed in a remote cabin in Tennessee, and director Sam Raimi put his cast through genuinely grueling conditions.
Cosmic horror, inspired by H.P. Lovecraft's writings, emphasizes humanity's insignificance against incomprehensible cosmic entities.
Boris Karloff's portrayal of the Monster in Frankenstein (1931) required over four hours of makeup application by Jack Pierce each day.
Get Out was released on February 24, 2017, and went on to win the Academy Award for Best Original Screenplay.
The Candyman (1992) explored themes of racism, urban legends, and gentrification through its setting in Chicago's Cabrini-Green housing projects.
KNB EFX Group, founded by Robert Kurtzman, Greg Nicotero, and Howard Berger, has created effects for over 400 films and TV shows including The Walking Dead.
Rosemary's Baby (1968) was filmed on location at the Dakota building in New York City, where John Lennon was later tragically killed.
A Nightmare on Elm Street (1984) was partly inspired by real news articles about Southeast Asian refugees who died in their sleep from unexplained causes.
The Shining (1980) required a record-breaking 127 takes for one scene with Shelley Duvall, which director Stanley Kubrick insisted on reshooting.
Paranormal Activity used Paramount's marketing budget to let audiences 'demand' screenings in their cities, creating viral grassroots buzz.
Midnight Mass (2021), created by Mike Flanagan, explored themes of faith, addiction, and mortality on a small isolated island.
The body horror subgenre, pioneered by David Cronenberg, focuses on the violation or transformation of the human body to create dread.
Bela Lugosi was buried in his Dracula cape at his own request after his death in 1956.
It (2017) became the highest-grossing horror film of all time at the point of its release, earning over $700 million worldwide.
The Omen was released on June 6, 1976 — 6/6/76 — intentionally chosen for its devilish numerology.
I Know What You Did Last Summer (1997) was based on a 1973 novel by Lois Duncan, who was unhappy with the film's slasher approach to her mystery story.
George Romero's zombie films served as social commentary, with Night of the Living Dead critiquing racism and Dawn of the Dead satirizing consumerism.
The Insidious franchise introduced the concept of 'The Further,' an astral plane where malevolent spirits dwell.
Ju-On: The Grudge (2002) popularized the onryō ghost archetype in Western culture, featuring spirits driven by rage and vengeance.
Servant, created by Tony Basgallop and produced by M. Night Shyamalan, ran for four seasons on Apple TV+ as a psychological horror thriller.
Alfred Hitchcock bought up as many copies of the Psycho source novel as he could to keep the ending a secret from audiences.
Barbarian (2022) was praised for its unpredictable plot structure, pivoting between multiple characters and timelines in surprising ways.
The werewolf film subgenre dates back to Werewolf of London (1935), six years before the more famous The Wolf Man (1941).
Scream was released on December 20, 1996, and revitalized the slasher genre by deconstructing its own tropes.
Smile (2022) was originally developed as a short film called Laura Hasn't Slept before being expanded into a feature.
The Hellraiser franchise is based on Clive Barker's novella The Hellbound Heart and features the iconic Cenobite villain Pinhead.
Toni Collette's performance in Hereditary was widely regarded as one of the best horror performances ever, though she was not nominated for an Oscar.
The Chucky TV series premiered on Syfy and USA Network in 2021, continuing the Child's Play franchise with original creator Don Mancini at the helm.
Folk horror, exemplified by The Wicker Man (1973), explores the terror of ancient rituals and pagan beliefs persisting in isolated communities.
John Carpenter's Halloween was the first film to use the Steadicam for a killer's point-of-view shot in its famous opening sequence.
Nope (2022) by Jordan Peele was a sci-fi horror film that explored themes of spectacle, exploitation, and Hollywood's treatment of animals.
The original Dracula (1931) starring Bela Lugosi was filmed simultaneously in English and Spanish versions using the same sets.
A Quiet Place (2018) was co-written by and starred real-life couple John Krasinski and Emily Blunt.
The Texas Chain Saw Massacre was inspired in part by the real crimes of Ed Gein, who also inspired Psycho and The Silence of the Lambs.
Us (2019) features Lupita Nyong'o in a dual role, and she developed two distinct voices for her characters Adelaide and Red.
The Exorcist was the first horror film to be nominated for the Academy Award for Best Picture.
Wes Craven originally envisioned Freddy Krueger as a child molester, but toned down the backstory due to real-world child abuse cases in the news.
The Blair Witch Project actors were given less and less food during filming to capture genuine frustration and desperation on camera.
Halloween (2018) brought back Jamie Lee Curtis as Laurie Strode 40 years after the original and grossed $255 million worldwide.
Dario Argento's Suspiria (1977) is considered a masterpiece of Italian horror, known for its vivid Technicolor palette and Goblin's prog-rock score.
The Conjuring (2013) was released on July 19, 2013, and earned over $319 million worldwide on a $20 million budget.
Vampire films date back to Nosferatu (1922), making vampires one of the oldest monster archetypes in cinema history.
Robert Englund played Freddy Krueger in eight films over 19 years, making the character one of the most iconic villains in horror.
Ari Aster's films Hereditary and Midsommar both explore grief and family trauma through the lens of escalating, disturbing horror.
The Scream franchise's Ghostface mask was based on Edvard Munch's painting The Scream and was found by producer Marianne Maddalena in a house during location scouting.
Night of the Living Dead (1968) accidentally entered the public domain because the distributor forgot to include a copyright notice on prints.
Hereditary was released on June 8, 2018, and was called 'this generation's Exorcist' by critics.
The original Paranormal Activity ending was changed at Steven Spielberg's suggestion after he watched a screener and offered notes.
The Walking Dead's Greg Nicotero, who leads the show's makeup effects, was a protégé of legendary effects artist Tom Savini.
Psychological horror relies on the characters' mental and emotional states rather than gore or monsters to create fear.
Jordan Peele won the Academy Award for Best Original Screenplay for Get Out on March 4, 2018.
The Shining's 'Here's Johnny!' line was improvised by Jack Nicholson and was not in the script.
Candyman's Tony Todd negotiated to be paid $1,000 for every bee sting he received during filming — he was stung 23 times.
Saw (2004) was shot in just 18 days and launched a billion-dollar franchise.
The Haunting of Hill House features a 17-minute single-take episode, 'Two Storms,' that required extensive choreography and rehearsal.
The Exorcist's director William Friedkin fired a gun on set to get genuine startled reactions from the cast.
Midsommar (2019) was released on July 3, 2019, and featured a director's cut that was nearly 30 minutes longer than the theatrical version.
The zombie genre was virtually dormant in mainstream cinema between Night of the Living Dead (1968) and Dawn of the Dead (1978).
In Friday the 13th Part 2 (1981), Jason Voorhees wore a burlap sack over his head — the iconic hockey mask did not appear until Part III.
Jamie Lee Curtis is often called the original 'scream queen' for her role in Halloween (1978), though the term existed earlier.
James Wan created both the Saw and Insidious franchises before directing the Conjuring universe, amassing billions at the global box office.
The original poster for The Exorcist, showing a man standing before a window of light, is one of the most iconic movie posters ever designed.
Wes Craven directed both A Nightmare on Elm Street (1984) and Scream (1996), bookending and reviving the slasher genre twice.
It Chapter Two (2019) featured Bill Hader, James McAvoy, and Jessica Chastain as the adult versions of the Losers' Club.
The Silence of the Lambs (1991) is one of only three films to win all five major Academy Awards — Best Picture, Director, Actor, Actress, and Screenplay.
The Blair Witch Project was released on July 30, 1999, and pioneered viral internet marketing with its fake documentary website.
American Horror Story has featured recurring actors like Sarah Paulson, Evan Peters, and Jessica Lange playing different characters each season.
The creature design of the Xenomorph from Alien (1979) was created by Swiss artist H.R. Giger, blending biomechanical and organic forms.
Poltergeist (1982) is associated with a supposed curse — several cast members died under unusual circumstances in the years following its release.
A Nightmare on Elm Street was released on November 9, 1984, and launched the career of Johnny Depp, who appeared in his first film role.
Mike Flanagan has become Netflix's go-to horror creator, directing The Haunting of Hill House, Bly Manor, Midnight Mass, and The Fall of the House of Usher.
The original Godzilla (1954) was a Japanese horror allegory for the atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki.
In Rosemary's Baby, director Roman Polanski insisted on real locations and naturalistic performances to make the horror feel grounded and believable.
The Evil Dead franchise spans five films, a TV series (Ash vs Evil Dead), and has become a cornerstone of horror-comedy.
Ghostface in the Scream series has been played by different killers in each installment, making the identity always a mystery.
The Conjuring 2 (2016) is set in Enfield, England, and is based on the real Enfield Poltergeist case from 1977.
Friday the 13th (1980) was released on May 9, 1980, and spawned twelve films, a TV series, novels, comic books, and video games.
The Witch (2015) director Robert Eggers sourced his dialogue from actual 17th-century journals, letters, and court documents.
Stanley Kubrick's The Shining diverged significantly from Stephen King's novel, leading King to publicly express his dissatisfaction with the adaptation.
Dawn of the Dead (1978) was filmed in the Monroeville Mall in Pennsylvania over four months, mostly shooting at night after the mall closed.
The original budget for Night of the Living Dead was just $114,000, raised by Romero and his friends pooling their own money.
Insidious (2010) was made for just $1.5 million and earned over $97 million worldwide, proving horror remains a profitable genre.
The term 'giallo' literally means 'yellow' in Italian, referring to the yellow covers of cheap mystery paperback novels popular in Italy.
Stranger Things draws heavily from the works of Stephen King, Steven Spielberg, and John Carpenter in its visual style and storytelling.
The Shining was released on May 23, 1980, and while initially receiving mixed reviews, it is now considered one of the greatest horror films ever made.
In the original Halloween, the character of Michael Myers is credited as 'The Shape' to emphasize his role as a faceless embodiment of evil.
Tom Savini designed the practical effects for Friday the 13th (1980), creating the graphic kills that became the franchise's signature.
The Orphanage (El Orfanato, 2007) is a Spanish horror film produced by Guillermo del Toro that blends ghost story elements with emotional drama.
Get Out's concept of the 'Sunken Place' became a widely used cultural metaphor for the marginalization of Black voices.
Hellraiser (1987) was written and directed by Clive Barker, based on his own novella The Hellbound Heart, and introduced the Cenobites to horror cinema.
Paranormal Activity was released on September 25, 2009, in wide release after a limited run that built massive word-of-mouth.
The 'jump scare' technique dates back to producer Val Lewton's Cat People (1942), which pioneered the 'Lewton Bus' — a sudden benign noise after tension.
Jordan Peele's Nope (2022) featured a creature that was deliberately designed to look like a UFO but was actually a living organism.
Freddy Krueger's glove with razor-tipped fingers was designed by Wes Craven to be something a child could make, reflecting the character's twisted creativity.
The Haunting of Bly Manor (2020) was Mike Flanagan's follow-up to Hill House, loosely adapting Henry James's The Turn of the Screw.
Child's Play creator Don Mancini has been involved in every Chucky film, making it one of the most creator-driven horror franchises.
In Alien (1979), the chestburster scene shocked the cast because director Ridley Scott did not tell them what would happen, capturing genuine terror.
The Final Destination franchise is built around elaborate Rube Goldberg-style death sequences where Death picks off survivors of a disaster.
John Carpenter's The Thing (1982) was a box office failure on release, partly because it opened two weeks after the family-friendly E.T. the Extra-Terrestrial.
The Babadook director Jennifer Kent studied experimental and art films to craft the surreal visual style of the monster.
Scream (1996) was written by Kevin Williamson, who conceived the story after watching a documentary about the Gainesville Ripper.
Dracula (1931) was released on February 12, 1931, and Bela Lugosi's performance defined the popular image of Count Dracula.
The 'torture porn' subgenre, exemplified by Hostel and Saw, became a dominant trend in mid-2000s horror and sparked significant debate.
Ari Aster's Hereditary features a miniature recreation of the family's home that took the art department weeks to build to scale.
The original Night of the Living Dead never uses the word 'zombie' — the creatures are referred to as 'ghouls.'
The Exorcist author William Peter Blatty based his novel on a 1949 exorcism case involving a young boy in Maryland.
It (2017) was released on September 8, 2017, and broke multiple box office records for horror and R-rated films.
The bird imagery in Hitchcock's The Birds (1963) was partially achieved by throwing live birds at actress Tippi Hedren during filming.
Halloween's budget was so low that many of the actors wore their own clothes, and the fake autumn leaves were recycled and repainted between scenes.
Ringu (1998) was based on Koji Suzuki's novel Ring and spawned a worldwide trend of Asian horror remakes in Hollywood.
In Midsommar, the flower dress worn by Florence Pugh's character in the finale weighs approximately 33 pounds.
George Romero turned down the chance to direct Resident Evil (2002), feeling the studio wanted too much creative control.
The horror convention circuit, including events like Monsterpalooza and HorrorHound, draws hundreds of thousands of fans annually.
Frankenstein (1931) was released on November 21, 1931, and Boris Karloff's portrayal of the Monster became instantly iconic.
The Conjuring's farmhouse set was built from scratch in North Carolina, and James Wan insisted on practical scares over CGI wherever possible.
Tobe Hooper went on to direct Poltergeist (1982) after The Texas Chain Saw Massacre, though Steven Spielberg's involvement led to a long-running directorial credit debate.
A Quiet Place Part II (2021) was one of the first major blockbusters released theatrically after the COVID-19 pandemic shutdowns.
The Black Phone (2022) was directed by Scott Derrickson and starred Ethan Hawke as a masked kidnapper called The Grabber.
John Carpenter was paid only $10,000 to direct Halloween, but negotiated a percentage of profits that earned him millions.
The zombie walk phenomenon, where large groups dress as zombies and shamble through cities, began in Sacramento in 2001 and spread worldwide.
Ready or Not (2019) blended horror with dark comedy as a bride must survive a deadly game of hide and seek with her new in-laws.
The Amityville Horror (1979) was based on Jay Anson's book about the allegedly haunted Lutz family home on Long Island.
Psycho (1960) was released on June 16, 1960, and Hitchcock demanded that theaters not admit anyone after the film had started.
The werewolf transformation in An American Werewolf in London set a new standard for practical effects and won the inaugural Best Makeup Oscar.
Annihilation (2018) blended sci-fi and body horror, featuring a biological entity called the Shimmer that mutates everything within its expanding boundary.
In The Shining, the carpet pattern in the Overlook Hotel has become one of the most analyzed and referenced design elements in film history.
The remake of Suspiria (2018), directed by Luca Guadagnino, divided audiences with its radical reimagining of Argento's 1977 original.
Halloween III: Season of the Witch (1982) is the only film in the Halloween franchise that does not feature Michael Myers.
James Wan's directorial debut was Saw (2004), which he co-created with Leigh Whannell while they were film students in Melbourne, Australia.
The Lighthouse (2019) by Robert Eggers was shot in black and white with a nearly square 1.19:1 aspect ratio to evoke a claustrophobic 19th-century feel.
Host (2020) was a found footage horror film made entirely over Zoom during the COVID-19 lockdown and was critically acclaimed.
The Rosemary's Baby apartment building, the Bramford, was a fictionalized version of the Dakota, one of Manhattan's most famous residences.
Candyman (2021) was a direct sequel to the 1992 original and explored gentrification and racial violence in modern-day Chicago.
Roger Corman produced dozens of low-budget horror films in the 1950s and 60s and served as a mentor to directors like Francis Ford Coppola and Martin Scorsese.
The Saw franchise's 'Jigsaw Killer,' John Kramer, technically never directly kills anyone — he creates elaborate traps and considers himself a teacher.
Smile (2022) was released on September 30, 2022, and its viral marketing campaign featured actors with unsettling smiles at live sporting events.
The vampire film Let the Right One In (2008) from Sweden is considered one of the best vampire films ever made, blending childhood loneliness with horror.
In A Nightmare on Elm Street, the scene where Freddy pushes through the wall above Nancy's bed was achieved by stretching spandex over the set wall.
Scream 2 (1997) opened with a movie-within-a-movie called 'Stab,' based on the events of the first film, adding another layer of meta-commentary.
The Descent (2005) was a British horror film set in an unmapped cave system, praised for its claustrophobic tension and female-led cast.
Hereditary's shocking car scene was kept out of all trailers and marketing to preserve its impact on audiences.
The Universal Monsters — Dracula, Frankenstein, The Mummy, The Wolf Man, and The Invisible Man — were Hollywood's first shared cinematic universe.
Night of the Living Dead was inducted into the National Film Registry by the Library of Congress in 1999 for being culturally significant.
The Texas Chain Saw Massacre was released on October 1, 1974, and was banned in several countries due to its intense violence.
M. Night Shyamalan's The Sixth Sense (1999) features one of cinema's most famous twist endings, and grossed $672 million worldwide.
The original Candyman (1992) was based on a short story by Clive Barker called 'The Forbidden,' which was set in Liverpool, not Chicago.
An American Werewolf in London (1981) blended horror and comedy in a way that influenced countless horror-comedies that followed.
The Exorcist spider-walk scene was filmed for the original 1973 release but cut — it was restored in the 2000 Director's Cut.
Wes Craven's New Nightmare (1994) broke the fourth wall by setting the story in the real world, predating Scream's meta approach by two years.
Train to Busan director Yeon Sang-ho also created the animated prequel Seoul Station (2016), which tells the story of the outbreak's beginning.
The Blair Witch Project's actors — Heather Donahue, Michael C. Williams, and Joshua Leonard — were listed as 'missing, presumed dead' on IMDb as part of the marketing.
Psycho's Norman Bates, played by Anthony Perkins, was partly inspired by real serial killer Ed Gein's relationship with his domineering mother.
Don't Breathe (2016) reversed the typical home invasion premise by making the intruders the protagonists trapped with a dangerous blind man.
John Carpenter considers The Thing his best film, even though it was initially panned by critics and audiences in 1982.
The Conjuring's Annabelle doll is based on a real Raggedy Ann doll that Ed and Lorraine Warren kept in a glass case in their occult museum.
Prom Night (1980) capitalized on Jamie Lee Curtis's scream queen status, casting her just two years after Halloween.
The original Scream mask was mass-produced by Fun World as a generic Halloween costume before it was discovered by the film's production team.
Jordan Peele's Us (2019) was released on March 22, 2019, and featured hidden references to Jeremiah 11:11 throughout the film.
The term 'elevated horror' emerged in the 2010s to describe arthouse horror films like The Witch, Hereditary, and Midsommar, though many filmmakers dislike the label.
Evil Dead II (1987) is both a sequel and semi-remake of the original, and is considered one of the greatest horror-comedies ever made.
In The Ring (2002), the American remake of Ringu, the cursed videotape was designed by combining multiple art films and experimental video techniques.
Rick Baker's makeup effects career spanned over 40 years and earned him seven Academy Awards, more than any other individual in that category.
The Texas Chainsaw Massacre 2 (1986) was a deliberate dark comedy, contrasting sharply with the dead-serious tone of the original.
Hammer Horror, the British production company, produced iconic horror films from the 1950s through 70s, starring Christopher Lee and Peter Cushing.
The Conjuring was released during a period when supernatural horror was dominating the box office, following the success of Insidious and Sinister.
Child's Play (1988) was released on November 9, 1988, and the animatronic Chucky doll required nine puppeteers to operate.
In Scream, Wes Craven makes a cameo as a school janitor wearing a Freddy Krueger-style sweater, a nod to his own creation.
The Purge franchise is built on the premise that all crime is legal for one night a year, using horror to explore socioeconomic inequality.
The Autopsy of Jane Doe (2016) set its entire story inside a morgue, creating claustrophobic tension from a single-location premise.
William Friedkin slapped actor Father William O'Malley in the face before filming a key scene in The Exorcist to get a more emotional performance.
It Follows (2014) used a retro synthesizer score and ambiguous time period to create an unsettling, dreamlike atmosphere.
The original Saw was inspired by creators James Wan and Leigh Whannell wanting to make a horror film cheaply — most of it takes place in one room.
Peeping Tom (1960) was so controversial upon release that it effectively ended director Michael Powell's career, though it's now considered a classic.
The Grudge (2004), the American remake starring Sarah Michelle Gellar, was directed by the original Ju-On director Takashi Shimizu.
The Shining's twins, played by Lisa and Louise Burns, were actually 12 years old during filming, not the younger age they appear.
Tobe Hooper's The Texas Chain Saw Massacre was initially rated X and had to be significantly edited to receive an R rating for wider release.
Truth or Dare (2018), Happy Death Day (2017), and other Blumhouse productions exemplify the studio's model of producing horror films for under $5 million.
The Exorcist was released in only 26 theaters initially, and lines stretched around city blocks for weeks.
Alfred Hitchcock never won an Academy Award for Best Director despite multiple nominations, including for Psycho and Rear Window.
Cabin in the Woods (2012), co-written by Joss Whedon, deconstructed horror tropes by revealing a secret organization controlling monster scenarios.
The Invisible Man (2020) reimagined the classic Universal monster as a domestic abuse thriller, starring Elisabeth Moss.
A Nightmare on Elm Street 3: Dream Warriors (1987) is widely considered the best sequel in the franchise, co-written by Frank Darabont.
Mike Flanagan's Doctor Sleep (2019) served as both a sequel to Kubrick's The Shining and an adaptation of Stephen King's novel, bridging two conflicting visions.
The original Ghostbusters (1984), while more comedy, draws heavily on horror imagery with its ghosts, demons, and apocalyptic climax.
Leatherface's mask in The Texas Chain Saw Massacre was made by the art department from layers of fiberglass and latex to appear as real human skin.
The Haunting (1963), directed by Robert Wise, is often cited as one of the scariest films ever made despite showing no ghosts on screen.
Annabelle (2014), a spinoff of The Conjuring, launched its own trilogy and expanded the Conjuring universe.
The Blair Witch Project directors Eduardo Sánchez and Daniel Myrick sent their actors into the woods with minimal guidance, capturing real fear and confusion.
In A Quiet Place, the sound design team recorded silence in multiple natural environments to create the film's oppressive quiet atmosphere.
The Fly (1986) by David Cronenberg is considered one of the greatest body horror films, with Jeff Goldblum's gradual transformation into a human-fly hybrid.
Scream (2022), the fifth entry in the franchise, was the first not directed by Wes Craven, who passed away in 2015.
Oculus (2013) was Mike Flanagan's breakthrough film, featuring a haunted mirror that distorts reality and perception.
Jennifer's Body (2009), initially dismissed as a flop, was later reappraised as a feminist horror film and developed a cult following.
The classic horror score for Psycho was composed by Bernard Herrmann using only string instruments, creating the iconic shrieking violins.
They Live (1988) by John Carpenter uses its sci-fi horror premise as a critique of Reagan-era consumerism and class inequality.

Nightmare on Elm Street (1984) was produced by New Line Cinema, which was then a small independent studio — the film's success was so massive it earned the company the nickname 'The House That Freddy Built.'
28 Days Later (2002) revitalized the zombie genre with fast-moving infected humans and was shot primarily on digital video for a gritty, documentary feel.
The Witch's Black Phillip, the family's goat who is revealed as the devil, became a viral internet sensation and meme.
Wes Craven held a master's degree in philosophy and writing from Johns Hopkins University before becoming a horror director.
In Hereditary, the clicking sound made by Charlie became an eerie signature that audiences would mimic in theaters.
The Exorcist III (1990), directed by original author William Peter Blatty, features a hallway scare considered one of the greatest jump scares in film history.
Carrie (1976), directed by Brian De Palma, was the first Stephen King novel adapted into a feature film.
Get Out was released during Black History Month 2017, and Jordan Peele has said the timing was intentional.
The Nightmare on Elm Street franchise earned over $450 million at the box office across its original run of films.
Sinister (2012) was named the scariest movie ever made by a scientific study that measured audience heart rates during viewing.
Alien (1979) was released on May 25, 1979, and its tagline — 'In space, no one can hear you scream' — became one of the most famous in movie history.
George Romero made six 'Dead' films over his career, from Night of the Living Dead (1968) to Survival of the Dead (2009).
The Strangers (2008) was inspired by the Manson Family murders and director Bryan Bertino's childhood memory of strangers knocking on his door.
The Omen (1976) production was plagued by eerie real-life incidents, including a crew member's plane being struck by lightning and an animal handler being killed by a tiger.
Blumhouse Productions, founded by Jason Blum, has revolutionized horror by producing low-budget films with massive returns, including Get Out, Paranormal Activity, and The Purge.
The original It miniseries aired on ABC on November 18, 1990, with Tim Curry's performance as Pennywise terrifying an entire generation.
Creepshow (1982) was a collaboration between George Romero and Stephen King, styled after EC Comics horror anthologies from the 1950s.
The Shining's Overlook Hotel was based on the Stanley Hotel in Estes Park, Colorado, where Stephen King stayed and was inspired to write the novel.
In Scream (1996), the opening call scene with Drew Barrymore was inspired by the real-life calls made by serial killers to their victims.
Night of the Demons (1988) and Return of the Living Dead (1985) are beloved cult horror films that blend scares with punk rock attitude.
The Conjuring: The Devil Made Me Do It (2021) was based on the controversial 1981 trial of Arne Cheyenne Johnson, who claimed demonic possession as a murder defense.
The Mist (2007) by Frank Darabont features one of the most devastating endings in horror history, which Stephen King said he wished he had written.
X (2022) by Ti West is set in 1979 and pays homage to The Texas Chain Saw Massacre while exploring themes of aging and desire.
The Grudge franchise spans American, Japanese, and South Korean films, with the curse of Kayako Saeki as the connecting thread.
Insidious Chapter 2 (2013) cleverly used time travel to recontextualize events from the first film.
Mandy (2018) starred Nicolas Cage in an ultraviolent psychedelic horror film that became an instant cult classic.
The Friday the 13th franchise includes a crossover film, Freddy vs. Jason (2003), which pitted two of horror's biggest icons against each other.
The original Candyman features Philip Glass's haunting organ score, unusual for a slasher film and lending it an operatic, tragic quality.
M3GAN (2023) became a viral sensation before release due to its dancing robot doll, spawning countless memes and TikTok videos.
Tobe Hooper also directed the original Salem's Lot TV miniseries (1979), adapting Stephen King's vampire novel for CBS.
The Wicker Man (1973) was released on December 1, 1973, and is considered the definitive folk horror film.
Clive Barker's Nightbreed (1990) was drastically re-edited by the studio against the director's wishes, and a director's cut was finally released in 2014.
The Scream franchise has earned over $900 million worldwide across its six films.
In The Conjuring, the clapping game sequence was conceived by James Wan as a way to build tension using only sound and practical effects.
Re-Animator (1985), loosely based on H.P. Lovecraft, is a cult classic known for its over-the-top gore and darkly comic tone.
Pearl (2022), Ti West's prequel to X, was written during the COVID-19 pandemic and filmed back-to-back with X in New Zealand.
The Amityville Horror franchise includes over 20 films, making it one of the longest-running horror series despite widely varying quality.
John Carpenter's Christine (1983) is an adaptation of Stephen King's novel about a possessed 1958 Plymouth Fury.
Wes Craven died on August 30, 2015, and the horror community mourned the loss of one of the genre's most influential voices.
Session 9 (2001) was filmed in a real abandoned mental asylum, the Danvers State Hospital in Massachusetts, adding genuine atmosphere.
Happy Death Day (2017) combined the slasher genre with Groundhog Day's time loop concept, creating a fresh take on the formula.
The scariest moment in The Exorcist — Regan's head spinning 360 degrees — required a specially built mechanical dummy and took days to prepare.
Suspiria (1977) was shot using the last Technicolor processing machine in Rome, giving the film its distinctive, oversaturated color palette.
Jordan Peele's production company, Monkeypaw Productions, has become one of the most prominent horror-focused studios in Hollywood.
The Howling (1981) was released the same year as An American Werewolf in London, leading to a werewolf film rivalry that year.
Danny Lloyd, who played Danny Torrance in The Shining, did not know he was making a horror film — Kubrick told him it was a drama.
The original Frankenstein (1931) featured a scene where the Monster throws a little girl into a lake, which was censored for decades.
Midsommar's central location, the Hårga commune, was built as a full practical set in Hungary, with custom-painted murals that foreshadow the plot.
The Craft (1996) blended teen drama with supernatural horror and became a cult classic that influenced Goth culture in the late 1990s.
Carrie (1976) was released on November 3, 1976, and featured the iconic prom scene where Sissy Spacek is drenched in pig's blood.
A24 has become a major force in horror cinema, distributing films like The Witch, Hereditary, Midsommar, X, and Talk to Me.
The original Poltergeist used real human skeletons in the swimming pool scene because they were cheaper than plastic props at the time.
Tucker and Dale vs. Evil (2010) brilliantly subverted hillbilly horror tropes by telling the story from the supposed villains' perspective.
The popularity of horror podcasts like The NoSleep Podcast and Last Podcast on the Left has created a new avenue for horror storytelling.
Candyman (1992) was released on October 16, 1992, and Tony Todd's performance as the hook-handed specter became iconic.
The Paranormal Activity franchise produced six films between 2007 and 2021, with a total franchise gross exceeding $890 million.
Stanley Kubrick reportedly called Stephen King at 3 AM during pre-production of The Shining to discuss whether ghost stories were optimistic because they implied an afterlife.
Talk to Me (2023) was directed by Australian YouTubers Danny and Michael Philippou in their feature film debut and became a critical and commercial hit.
The Nightingale (2018) by Babadook director Jennifer Kent is a brutal revenge film set in 1825 colonial Tasmania.
In the original Friday the 13th, the final jump scare of young Jason leaping from the lake was a last-minute addition inspired by the ending of Carrie.
The Exorcist's 'Captain Howdy' subliminal face flash appears for only a few frames, but audiences reported feeling deeply unsettled without knowing why.
Event Horizon (1997) blended sci-fi with haunted house horror, and its missing footage — reportedly too disturbing to keep — has become the stuff of legend.
The Nun (2018), set in 1952 Romania, is the earliest chronological entry in The Conjuring universe timeline.
Malignant (2021), directed by James Wan, surprised audiences with its wild third-act twist that turns the film into over-the-top action horror.
The success of horror films at the box office has consistently outpaced other genres relative to production costs, making horror one of the most reliable investments in Hollywood.
Rosemary's Baby was released on June 12, 1968, and is considered a landmark of psychological horror filmmaking.
The Hellraiser franchise spans ten films, with a Hulu reboot in 2022 featuring a female Pinhead played by Jamie Clayton.
Bone Tomahawk (2015) blended western and horror genres, featuring one of the most graphic death scenes in modern horror.
The original Dawn of the Dead (1978) was released unrated because Romero refused to cut it for an R rating.
Mike Flanagan's Gerald's Game (2017) was considered unfilmable for years because most of the novel takes place inside the protagonist's mind.
The phrase 'Be afraid. Be very afraid' originated from the marketing for The Fly (1986) and entered common usage.
Fright Night (1985) is a beloved vampire comedy-horror that was remade in 2011 with Colin Farrell.
The Green Room (2015) featured Patrick Stewart playing against type as a violent neo-Nazi club owner.
Terrifier 2 (2022) made over $10 million at the box office despite having no traditional studio distribution, becoming a grassroots horror hit.
The Strangers (2008) was released on May 30, 2008, with the tagline 'Because you were home,' emphasizing the randomness of the home invasion.
Megan Fox starred in Jennifer's Body (2009), written by Diablo Cody, which has been reclaimed as a feminist allegory about female rage and exploitation.
The Suspiria dance academy sequences were choreographed with movements designed to suggest witchcraft rituals.
George Romero's influence on the zombie genre was so profound that he is often called 'The Father of the Zombie Film.'
In Child's Play, Brad Dourif has voiced Chucky in every film since 1988, spanning over 35 years in the role.
The Fall of the House of Usher (2023) by Mike Flanagan adapted multiple Edgar Allan Poe stories into a single interconnected narrative on Netflix.
Saw was released on October 29, 2004, just before Halloween, which became a tradition for the franchise's annual releases.
The Amityville Horror house at 112 Ocean Avenue still stands today, though the address was changed to deter tourists.
Tippi Hedren suffered a breakdown during filming of The Birds after Hitchcock used real birds instead of the mechanical ones he had promised.
The Hills Have Eyes (1977) by Wes Craven was loosely inspired by the legend of Sawney Bean, a medieval Scottish cannibal clan leader.
Paranormal Activity director Oren Peli shot the entire film in his own house over the course of a week.
The Curse of La Llorona (2019) is technically part of The Conjuring universe, connected through the character of Father Perez.
Sam Raimi developed his signature fast-moving camera style, dubbed 'shaky-cam,' during the making of The Evil Dead using a wooden plank.
The original Nosferatu's Count Orlok was played by Max Schreck, whose surname means 'terror' or 'fright' in German.
In Jaws (1975), the mechanical shark malfunctioned so often that Spielberg was forced to suggest the shark rather than show it, making the film more terrifying.
The Ritual (2017) is a British folk horror film set in the forests of northern Sweden, based on Adam Nevill's novel.
Audition (1999) by Takashi Miike is a Japanese horror film that lulls viewers with a slow romantic setup before erupting into extreme violence.
Scream VI (2023) moved the setting from Woodsboro to New York City for the first time in the franchise.
The Witch was the directorial debut of Robert Eggers and won the Best Director prize at the 2015 Sundance Film Festival.
The Babadook's pop-up book prop was so popular that the filmmakers had to produce actual copies for fans who wanted to buy it.
Drag Me to Hell (2009) marked Sam Raimi's return to horror after years directing the Spider-Man trilogy.
The original Suspiria featured sets designed with intentionally oversized door handles to make the actresses look childlike and vulnerable.
An Inconvenient Truth producer Lawrence Bender also produced horror films early in his career, including Quentin Tarantino's connections to the genre.
Sleepaway Camp (1983) is famous for its shocking twist ending, which remains one of the most discussed reveals in slasher history.
Bone-chilling sound effects in horror films are often created using everyday objects — celery snapping mimics bones breaking, and wet chamois leather sounds like skin tearing.
The Grudge series popularized the 'death rattle' croaking sound, which became one of the most recognizable audio cues in 2000s horror.
Cronenberg's Videodrome (1983) predicted the blurring of reality and media decades before the internet age, exploring how screens consume identity.
Hocus Pocus (1993) was a box office disappointment on release but became one of the most-watched Halloween films through annual TV airings.
The Suspiria (2018) remake featured Tilda Swinton in three roles, including an elderly male psychiatrist — a fact kept secret until release.
The original Texas Chain Saw Massacre dinner scene took over 26 hours of continuous filming in sweltering heat with rotting food on the table.
Pulse (Kairo, 2001) is a Japanese horror film about ghosts invading the world through the internet, predating modern digital horror trends.
The Void (2016) is a Canadian cosmic horror film that used almost entirely practical creature effects as a throwback to 1980s horror.
From Dusk Till Dawn (1996), written by Quentin Tarantino and directed by Robert Rodriguez, famously shifts from crime thriller to vampire horror midway through.
The Craft: Legacy (2020) served as a sequel to the 1996 original, continuing the franchise 24 years later.
In The Silence of the Lambs, Anthony Hopkins appears on screen for only about 16 minutes yet won the Academy Award for Best Actor.
Deep Red (Profondo Rosso, 1975) by Dario Argento is considered one of the finest Italian giallo films, blending mystery with graphic violence.
Trick 'r Treat (2007) was shelved by Warner Bros. for two years before becoming a massive cult hit on home video.
The Ring's cursed videotape imagery was influenced by the experimental films of Maya Deren and other surrealist filmmakers.
Final Destination 5 (2011) features an ending that cleverly loops back to the events of the first film, surprising longtime fans.
Gremlins (1984) and Poltergeist (1982) were among the films that led to the creation of the PG-13 rating in 1984.
The Wailing (2016) is a South Korean horror film that blends supernatural terror with dark mystery in a rural village setting.
Cloverfield (2008) applied the found footage technique to a giant monster movie, creating a unique survival horror experience.
The Crazies (1973), one of George Romero's lesser-known films, depicted a government bioweapon driving a town insane and was remade in 2010.
Horror film soundtracks frequently use infrasound — frequencies below 20 Hz that humans can't consciously hear but can feel, inducing unease and anxiety.
Sweeney Todd (2007), directed by Tim Burton, brought the horror-musical hybrid to mainstream audiences with graphic throat-slitting scenes.
Colin (2008), a British zombie film, was famously made for just £45, following the zombie apocalypse from the zombie's perspective.
Gerald's Game (2017) by Mike Flanagan features a harrowing degloving scene that caused some Netflix viewers to reportedly pass out.
The Haunting of Bly Manor pays homage to multiple Henry James stories, not just The Turn of the Screw, weaving them into one narrative.
Host (2020) was one of the few horror films shot entirely remotely during lockdown and earned a rare 100% score on Rotten Tomatoes.
Scanners (1981) by David Cronenberg features one of cinema's most iconic head explosion scenes, achieved entirely with practical effects.
The Nightmare on Elm Street house at 1428 North Genesee Avenue in Los Angeles is a real residential property that fans frequently visit.
Unfriended (2014) was one of the first 'screenlife' horror films, with the entire story playing out on a computer screen.
House of 1000 Corpses (2003) was Rob Zombie's directorial debut, and its sequel The Devil's Rejects (2005) was widely considered superior.
The Cabin in the Woods (2012) was filmed in 2009 but shelved for three years due to MGM's financial troubles before Lionsgate released it.
Don't Breathe (2016) was set in a decaying neighborhood of Detroit, which the director chose for its visual atmosphere of urban decay and isolation.
Frankenstein (1931) director James Whale was an openly gay filmmaker in 1930s Hollywood, and his outsider status informed his sympathetic portrayal of the Monster.
The original Candyman (1992) was shot on location at the real Cabrini-Green housing projects in Chicago with cooperation from actual residents.
Underwater (2020) was essentially an Alien-style horror film set at the bottom of the Mariana Trench, with Lovecraftian undertones.
The Neon Demon (2016) by Nicolas Winding Refn applied horror aesthetics to the fashion industry, exploring beauty, vanity, and predation.
Martyrs (2008) is a French extremity film that shifts from a home invasion thriller to philosophical body horror about transcendence through suffering.
Lamb (2021), an Icelandic folk horror film, features a couple raising a mysterious human-lamb hybrid child in remote rural Iceland.
Henry: Portrait of a Serial Killer (1986) was completed in 1986 but not released until 1990 due to its disturbing content, earning an X rating.
Slash film conventions like Friday the 13th conventions and Texas Frightmare Weekend have become major events for horror memorabilia collectors.
The menu from the dinner scene in The Texas Chain Saw Massacre inspired themed restaurants and haunted house attractions across the country.
Wrong Turn (2003) launched a franchise of six films featuring inbred cannibals in the West Virginia wilderness.
The Conjuring 2 (2016) was released on June 10, 2016, and James Wan came back to direct after initially saying he was done with the franchise.
Prey (2022), a Predator prequel set in 1719 among Comanche people, was praised for reinventing the franchise with a fresh perspective.
Bram Stoker's novel Dracula (1897) was partly inspired by Vlad the Impaler and the author's research into Eastern European folklore.
Crawl (2019), produced by Sam Raimi, trapped its characters in a flooded crawlspace with alligators during a hurricane.
Rob Zombie's Halloween (2007) remake divided fans with its extended backstory for Michael Myers, giving the famously unknowable killer a troubled childhood.
The Conjuring universe films follow a specific internal timeline spanning from 1952 (The Nun) through the 1980s (Annabelle Comes Home).
Possessor (2020) by Brandon Cronenberg, son of David Cronenberg, continued his father's legacy of cerebral body horror filmmaking.
The Fear Street trilogy (2021) on Netflix adapted R.L. Stine's book series into three connected films set across different time periods.
